title: Drymocallis geoides
taxonomy: Drymocallis geoides is a species of flowering plant in the rose family (Rosaceae). It is native to western North America. The classification of Drymocallis geoides is as follows: its kingdom is Plantae, its phylum is Magnoliophyta, its class is Magnoliopsida, its order is Rosales, its family is Rosaceae, its genus is Drymocallis, and its species is D. geoides. Drymocallis geoides is further divided into several subspecies. This classification helps to understand the plant's evolutionary relationships and characteristics.
uses: Drymocallis geoides, also known as creeping cinquefoil, is a plant with potential medicinal uses. The plant's roots, leaves, and stems are used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments. In some cultures, it is used to treat digestive issues, fever, and infections. Its roots are also believed to have anti-inflammatory properties, making it a potential treatment for wounds and skin conditions. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness and safety of using creeping cinquefoil as a medicinal plant.

parts: Drymocallis geoides, also known as Creeping Buckthorn or Western Buckthorn, is not typically documented for its medicinal parts. preparations: Drymocallis geoides is also known as golden creeping barberry or sticky cinquefoil. The plant's roots, leaves, and stems are used in herbal preparations, often in traditional medicine. intro: Drymocallis geoides, also known as the creeping Oregon grape, is a perennial plant species in the barberry family (Rosoideae) native to western North America. The roots and rhizomes of Drymocallis geoides have been used in traditional medicine for their potential health benefits, including the treatment of digestive issues, inflammation, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of this plant include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Drymocallis geoides include root extracts, teas, and tinctures, which are often used to support liver and digestive health, as well as to aid in the treatment of skin conditions such as eczema and acne.
